PARAGRAPH 1
Here is a python function designed to help researchers analyze historical dates efficiently. The code filters a list of date strings to return only those belonging to a specific century. It handles the logic that the nineteenth century spans from 1801 through 1900. This approach ensures accurate extraction for large datasets without unnecessary computational overhead. The algorithm parses the year from each string and compares it against the calculated start and end years for the target century. No complex date libraries are required for this simple arithmetic check. We assume input strings follow the standard ISO format strictly. The function returns a new list containing valid entries. This solution is optimized for speed and readability. It avoids importing heavy modules like pandas or datetime for basic year extraction. Instead, it uses string slicing to isolate the four-digit year component. This method is fast because it operates directly on the string data. The logic calculates the start year as one hundred times the century minus ninety nine. The end year is simply one hundred times the century. For example, century nineteen yields a range of 1801 to 1900. Any date with a year outside this range is excluded immediately. The code iterates through the input list once, making it linear in time complexity. This ensures scalability even with millions of records. Researchers can easily integrate this snippet into larger data processing pipelines. The function accepts a list of strings and an integer as arguments. It returns a list of strings matching the criteria. Error handling is minimal since the prompt assumes valid input formats. If a string is malformed, the slicing might fail, but we assume clean data. The implementation focuses on the core filtering logic. It does not modify the original list but creates a fresh one. This preserves the integrity of the source data. The code is written in standard python syntax compatible with version three. It uses list comprehension for concise and efficient iteration. The variable names are descriptive to aid understanding. Comments explain the century calculation clearly. This makes the code maintainable for future updates. The solution addresses the specific requirement of the nineteenth century example. It correctly includes 1801 and 1899 while excluding 1789 and 1900. The logic holds true for any integer century provided. One can test it with the twentieth century to verify the range 1901 to 2000. The function is robust and ready for production use in historical analysis tasks. It provides a quick way to segment data by time periods. This is essential
